parties: Paul Daryl Bachynski, Petitioner, and Michael Gallagher, Warden of William Head Institution, Robert Lusk, Warden of Kent Institution, and Deputy Commissioner of the Correctional Service of Canada, Pacific Region, Respondents
citation: [1995] B.C.J. No. 1715 DRS 95-15835 Victoria Registry No. 2533/95
court: British Columbia Supreme Court Victoria, British Columbia Bouck J.
body_html:
Heard: July 24-25, 1995.
Judgment: filed July 27, 1995 (16 pp.).

Prisons - Administration - Powers re prisoners - Transfers.

Application for an order in the nature of habeas corpus. The applicant was an inmate at a medium security prison when the prison authorities transferred him to a maximum security prison. The decision on the applicant's involuntary transfer was based on information received from inmates connecting him to the assault of one fellow inmate and the murder of another. While two inmates had been charged in that murder, investigation by the police which had not been completed at the time the present application was heard. Having exhausted his appeal remedies within the Corrections system, the applicant brought the present application essentially seeking to undo the transfer. In the course of pursuing those remedies, the respondent, at each of the three stages, made submissions both personally and by counsel. The sole issue was whether the conduct of the prison authorities was patently unreasonable.

HELD: Application dismissed. Courts were required to be vigilant in protecting the rights of a prisoner dealt with in an unreasonable manner. In this case the court was satisfied that the conduct of the respondents in replying to the evidence and the submissions of the petitioner was fair and reasonable.

Statutes, Regulations and Rules Cited:

Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, 1982, s. 7.

Counsel for the Petitioner: Vaughan L. Barrett.
Counsel for the Respondents: Martha E. McNeely.

BOUCK J.:

INTRODUCTION

Until 6 February 1995, Mr. Bachynski was an inmate at William Head Penitentiary (Medium Security) On that date the prison authorities transferred him to Kent Penitentiary (Maximum Security). He exhausted his appeal remedies on 1 May 1995. He now seeks an order in the nature of habeas corpus with certiorari in aid transferring him back to William Head Penitentiary.

The Respondents contend the transfer was reasonable in the circumstances.

FACTS

In September 1986, the Petitioner began serving a life sentence for first degree murder. He is eligible to apply for parole after serving 25 years. On 5 October 1992, prison officials transferred him from Matsqui Institution, near Abbotsford B.C. to William Head Institution, Vancouver Island, Metchosin, B.C.

On 15 November 1994, two other William Head inmates, Mr. Victor Lowe and Mr. Bryant Kertesz, allegedly assaulted another inmate, Mr. James Kelly. He suffered four serious puncture wounds. On 16 November 1994, Mr. Lowe and Mr. Kertesz were placed in a segregation unit at the William Head prison. On 18 November 1994 Mr. Lowe and Mr. Kertesz were transferred from William Head to a higher security penitentiary. Neither prison authorities or the police ever charged them with the alleged assault on Mr. Kelly.

On 17 November 1994, inmate Gary Garnet was murdered at William Head. Later, police charged inmates Cecil Lavery and Mathew Macdonald with the murder of Mr. Garnet. They were transferred to Kent Institution.

Evidence from the Crown, contained in an RCMP affidavit in support of a search warrant dated 28 November 1994, states that following the murder a person called Wright heard Lavery and Macdonald say, "Let's go tell Paul." Wright then saw Lavery and Macdonald walk from their Housing Unit, E-7 towards the Housing Unit where the Petitioner lived, E-4. Other confidential information allegedly placed the Petitioner with Lavery and Macdonald at the time of Mr. Garnet's murder. The affidavit also alleged that during the early morning hours of 18 November 1994, the police located a fresh blood spot on the walkway leading from Housing Unit E-7 to Housing Unit E-4. They also found two blood spots on the inside stairway leading to the area of the Petitioner's bedroom in Housing Unit E-4.

On 18 November, the Petitioner and a number of other inmates were placed in a segregation unit at William Head. On 16 December 1994, prison authorities served the Petitioner with a "Notice of Involuntary Transfer Recommendation." It recommended he be transferred to a maximum security institution for his alleged involvement in the assault on Mr. Kelly and the murder of Mr. Garnet. The Petitioner replied to this recommendation personally and through counsel.

On 20 December 1994, Crown Counsel wrote counsel for the Petitioner that "no charges are being contemplated against Mr. Bachynski." On 23 December 1994, the Warden confirmed his original recommendation after reviewing the Petitioner's objections.

On 18 January 1995, the William Head Warden issued a "Notification of Review or Recommendation Relative to Transfer" stating that he intended to recommend the Petitioner's involuntary transfer to Kent. It included additional information concerning the allegations against the Petitioner. The Petitioner appealed to the Assistant Deputy Commissioner.

On 2 February 1995, the Assistant Deputy Commissioner, Mr. Peterson approved the Warden's recommendation for the Petitioner's involuntary transfer from William Head to Kent. The Petitioner appealed this decision to the Regional Deputy Commissioner at the second level of appeal. On 6 February 1995 the Petitioner was transferred to Kent.

On 16 February 1995, Mr. T Sawatsky, the Regional Deputy Commissioner, denied the appeal at the second level.

From this decision the Petitioner took a final or third level appeal to the Commissioner, Mr. John Rama. On 1 May 1995 he also denied the appeal in these words:

Your grievance has been reviewed at the third level. Please accept my apologies for the delay in responding.

I have reviewed all relevant documentation and, given your suspected involvement in two serious incidents at William Head, one of which is currently under investigation by the RCMP, I support the decision to transfer you to higher security until such time as the investigation has been completed. Depending on the outcome, a final decision with regard to your transfer will be taken, You claim that your behaviour in William Head was positive. This fact is acknowledged by the Warden of that institution, however, this does not negate the fact that serious allegations have been made with regard to your involvement in the incidents and these must be taken seriously for the safety and security of the institution. They will remain so until such time as the extent of your involvement can be determined.

On 20 June 1995, the RCMP advised counsel for the Petitioner that "exhibits from the scene ... are still undergoing DNA testing. As such, our investigation is still very much ongoing and we are unable to conclude that Mr. Bachynski was not involved; ..." On 26 June 1995, Crown Counsel advised counsel for the Petitioner that the RCMP investigation was not complete.

It seems that when the William Head Warden made the original charges against the Petitioner on 16 December 1994, the only evidence against the him, with respect to the assault on Mr. Kelly, was an observation by an unknown person. Apparently that person suggested that Mr. Lowe and Mr. Kertesz allegedly sought out the Petitioner after the assault on Mr. Kelly. The Petitioner gave them something and they all slapped hands. The observer took the gesture to be of a congratulatory nature.

In his Notice of Recommendation of 16 December 1994, the Warden articulated the allegations concerning the involvement of the Petitioner in the murder of Mr. Garnet. He stated a trail of blood was found to lead directly from the crime scene to the threshold of the Petitioner's room in a William Head Living Unit. He went on to say that other information was still being analyzed which suggested the actual presence of the Petitioner at the crime scene either during or immediately after the murder. That included the discovery of a substance which appeared to be blood on a pair of shoes seized by the RCMP.

In addition, the Warden stated that intelligence information indicated the Petitioner was fulfilling a very powerful directive role within the institutional population. Prison authorities believed he was at the top of an inmate power structure with important issues and problems being vetted, sanctioned or authorized by him.

The Transfer decision of the Assistant Deputy Commissioner, Mr. Peterson dated 2 February 1995 repeated these allegations. After reviewing the findings of the Warden and the material filed by the Petitioner, Mr. Peterson stated he was satisfied with the response of the Warden to the Petitioner's requests for further information. As of 2 February 1995, Mr. Peterson was aware that the Crown was not considering charges against the Petitioner with respect to the murder of Mr. Garnet at that time. Nonetheless, he had been assured that the investigation was ongoing and the Petitioner was a suspect.

Given the serious nature of the allegations made against the Petitioner, Mr. Peterson concluded that the Petitioner's presence at William Head constituted an undue risk to the safety and security of the institution and the safety of other inmates. They were again upheld at the second level of appeal by Mr. Sawatsky on 16 February 1995. Finally, they were upheld at the third and final appeal level on 1 May 1995 by the Commissioner Mr. Rama.

Evidence filed with the court from other prison officials pre-dating the incidents of 15 and 17 November 1994 describe the Petitioner in favourable terms. On 7 November 1988, a Performance Report said he worked well on his own with little or no supervision. He was punctual and cooperative with staff and other inmates. On 2 June 1990 an officer at Kent Institution said he had made a remarkable change, he had a mature outlook on his life and he was a good influence on younger inmates. As of 8 November 1993, a Unit Manager at William Head said he had excellent work reports, he got along well with people, he was upfront with case management and he contributed to an overall sense of stability in his neighbourhood. A Progress Summary dated 15 December 1994 states that for the 2 years the Petitioner was in William Head he was relatively non-problematic.

At the time of these incidents, the Petitioner had served 7 1/2 years of his 25 year sentence. Apparently, he shot another man in some sort of a dispute between bike clubs. He had 14 previous convictions between 1969 and 1980. Four of these involved crimes of violence. His counsel argues that the effect of his transfer to Kent from William Head could seriously harm his chances for parole when his application for judicial review is heard after he serves 15 years of his sentence: Criminal Code, s.745.

On 11 July 1995, Mr. Bachynski filed this Petition. He seeks an order for relief in the nature of habeas corpus with certiorari in aid. If he succeeds he wants this court to order him transferred back from Kent, Maximum Security to William Head, Medium Security.

ISSUE

Was the conduct of the Respondents patently unreasonable in the circumstances?

ANALYSIS

First of all, the Respondent's do not contest the availability of the remedy in this case. A convenient summary of the law can be found in Balian v. Regional Transfer Board (1988), 62 C.R. (3d) 258 at 260-262 (Ontario Supreme Court [High Court of Justice]. Certiorari in aid is a remedy in the nature of an order allowing this court to make the remedy of habeas corpus more effective by requiring production of the record of the proceedings in the tribunal below. That order was not sought in these proceedings. Certiorari to quash is a remedy in the nature of an order setting aside the decision of a tribunal. It is not available to a provincial superior court where it is directed at a federal tribunal because the granting of such an order is within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Federal Court.

Nonetheless, on an application for habeas corpus, a court may inquire into the detention of a prisoner and determine whether it occurred according to law. If I find the transfer of the Petitioner from William Head to Kent was not done according to law, he may by order of the court be transferred back to William Head.

It is worth noting in summary form the process that Correctional officials followed in arriving at the result:

At each step along the way the Petitioner was given the opportunity to make written submissions by himself or through his counsel or both.

The major argument of counsel for the Petitioner was to the effect that viewed objectively, the conduct of the Respondents was patently unreasonable. Primarily, he bases his complaint on the failure of the Respondents to make additional investigations and answer more specifically the questions asked by him.

In substance, counsel for the Respondents say the Commissioner and other officials took all the necessary factors into account. They could not reveal the names of informers, or facts that might lead to the discovery of those names, since that could put the informers in jeopardy. The statute does not make the penitentiary officials investigating magistrates for the Petitioner. Nor do they act as judges who are bound by strict rules of procedure and evidence. They are not required to hold an oral inquiry so that witnesses may be examined and cross-examined. They need only act reasonably.

Both counsel referred to a total of about 40 cases. They carefully argued and dissected almost every one. The principle of fairness is the predominate feature in each of them. But fairness depends in large part on the facts in issue. Comparing the facts in every case cited by counsel to the facts in this case might be an interesting exercise, however, it is unlikely that it will yield an obvious answer. All I can do is try and extract the significant principles and apply them to the above facts.

The transfer of an inmate from one penal institution to another where his or activities are more severely confined, is a restriction on the inmates freedom: Trono v. Gallant (1989), 68 C.R. (3d) 173 (Fed. C.A.) at 180 (Pratte J.). Therefore, the transfer can also be reviewed under s.7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms (The Charter). It reads:

Everyone has the right of life, liberty and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice.

In these types of proceedings, the phrase "fundamental justice" means "fairness": Camphaug v. Canada (1990), 34 F.T.R. 165 at 166 (Fed. Ct.), Strayer J. His Lordship went on to say at page 167:

I fully accept the view of Marceau J ... that a decision to transfer is not like a conviction for an offence: what is required on the part of the decision maker is a reasonable belief that the prisoner should be moved for the sake of the orderly and proper administration of the institution. This implies that fairness in the making of such a decision does not require that the inmate be given all the particulars of the alleged wrongdoings, it is sufficient if he can make representations to demonstrate that the recommendation that he be moved is an unreasonable one.

The Petitioner complains that the Respondents did not say why they rejected his submissions. He contends, the Respondents were required to give an explanation: Lee and Mathieson v. Deputy Commissioner, [1993] F.C.J. No. 759, Federal Court of Canada, T-344-93, 28 July 1993 at pages 10-11:

It is insufficient to simply say that responses have been considered. If statements of the applicants in their submissions are not believed, then some explanation as to why they are not believed must be given. The obligation to consider submissions is not satisfied by a mere assertion to that effect.

With respect, I disagree. There may be situations where the facts of a particular incident cry out for a detailed explanation from penitentiary officials. But as a general principle this imposes too onerous a standard. First and foremost the Respondents are busy administrators. They are not trained lawyers or judges with little else to do but spend their days writing decisions. They are not administering the criminal law. As Dickson J. said in Service Employees' International Union Local No. 333 v. Nipawin District Staff Nurses Association (1973), 41 D.L.R. (3d) 6 at 13 (S.C.C.):

A tribunal is not required to make an explicit written finding on each constituent element, however subordinate, leading to its final conclusion.

It seems the Respondents based their decisions on the belief that the Petitioner should be moved for the sake of the orderly and proper administration of William Head Institution. That is an administrative matter. Only rarely will the courts interfere unless it is manifest the authorities did not deal properly with the inmate: Faulkner v. Canada (Solicitor General) et al (1992), 62 F.T.R. 19 at 24 [17], Jerome A.C.J.:

... the decision to transfer an inmate from one institution to another, is essentially an administrative matter. As such, it will only be interfered with by the courts on the rare occasion when it is manifestly clear the inmate has not been dealt with fairly, taking into account all the circumstances surrounding the transfer.

Behind these words is the realization that a prison is a complex institution. Some inmates resent their incarceration and do not intend to reform. Others are remorseful in varying degrees and want to rehabilitate themselves. Inmate secrecy, deceit and a code of silence are part of every day life within the institution. Inmate loyalty is usually given to those who control the inmate power structure. Very rarely is there explicit evidence of misbehaviour coming from credible sources within the inmate population. These observations are not new revelations. Most, if not all, are confirmed in the various cases relating to prisoners rights.

Prison authorities must act on the best information available. Sometimes it is unreliable and an individual inmate is wrongly charged or wrongly transferred. But given the nature of the inmate population and the necessity of preserving order there is often no other alternative. If a dangerous person is allowed to remain in the general prison population of a medium security institution, it is the other prisoners and staff who suffer the consequences because they have no place else to go.

Perhaps the Petitioner was not dealt with perfectly. But, the law does not demand perfection. This is because the system is run by human beings. Very rarely do any of us perform anything perfectly. Courts must always be vigilant in protecting the individual rights of an inmate who is dealt with in an unreasonable manner. On the other hand, we should realize the difficult situations that confront prison officials.

Looking at the overall treatment given to the Petitioner, I am satisfied the conduct of the Respondents in replying to the evidence and the submissions of the Petitioner was fair and reasonable in the circumstances. Even in his final decision of 1 May 1995, the Commissioner holds out the possibility of the Petitioner's transfer back to William Head once all the investigations are complete.

JUDGMENT

1. The petition is dismissed.
2. Costs follow the event.

BOUCK J.
